posts.text: Let's look at this (objectively unhinged) email that MrBeast employees got the other day, **from an internal comms lens.** AS AN INTERNAL COMMS MESSAGE, it is, unfortunately, good: + It is very clear! + It avoids jargon. It's written conversationally (and, most likely, by a human. Key for trust) + It shows employees one way to see themselves in the business and its success + It answers the crucial question "what's in it for me" + It's equitable ("no one is exempt from this") Obviously, however, its content is wildly inappropriate. + And possibly illegal? + Just to spell it out: Monitoring what the CEO eats is not, in fact, everyone's job. Bringing food to the CEO is not, in fact, everyone's job + "While working it is no longer your personal life" IN RELATION TO WHAT YOU'RE EATING is truly off the charts
posts.hook: Let's look at this (objectively unhinged) email that MrBeast employees got the other day, **from an internal comms lens.** AS AN INTERNAL CO…
posts.name: Liz Ryan

posts.text: "Remote workers are grabbing groceries or at the vet 30% of the time." Real quote. Real CEO. This week. His proof: a packed office at 5pm on a Friday. Friday. 5pm. The hour everyone stays late because leadership hasn't left yet. I've managed a $4.8M portfolio from 24 countries. Never met most of my team in person. 130% NRR. Groceries? Never. Surf? Yes. Results? Always. He had a full week of data. He went with bodies in chairs. Attendance theatre. The CEOs still counting heads at 5pm are telling on themselves. What's the most out-of-touch thing a leader has said about remote work?
posts.hook: "Remote workers are grabbing groceries or at the vet 30% of the time." Real quote.
posts.name: Liz Ryan

posts.text: The US is not a leader when it comes to workforce policies – in fact, we have the worst employment laws of any industrialized country. Congratulations to working people in Mexico on these tremendous labor reforms!

posts.text: Most LinkedIn CEO follow lists recycle the same 10 names. I wanted to build something different. I compiled a directory of 50 CEOs globally who are actually posting original content on LinkedIn in 2026, writing from real conviction, and changing how people in their industries think. Not just the mega-followed tech giants. The full picture. Satya Nadella on AI and empathy. Strive Masiyiwa on building African infrastructure across four decades. Hamdi Ulukaya on refugees, food, and what it actually means to put people first. Whitney Wolfe Herd on what female founders face that no one else does. Fei-Fei Li on where AI is actually going. And 45 more. The list spans tech, sustainability, purpose, scaling, and global voices from Africa, Asia, Europe, the Middle East, and Latin America. I applied one filter above all others: does this person post content that would genuinely change how you think about leadership and business? Every person on this list passed. Instead of recycling the same handful of names that appear on every list, I wanted to surface the leaders who genuinely deserve to be just as well known. Here are some of the leaders who made the list: Jensen Huang, Sundar Pichai, Tobias Lütke, Shishir Mehrotra, Anupam Mittal, Arianna Huffington, Indra Nooyi, Fidji Simo, Sallie Krawcheck, Sara Blakely, Julie Chapon, Marina Byezhanova, Hamdi Ulukaya, Strive Masiyiwa, Paul Polman, Marc Benioff, Alexandr Wang, Leena Nair, Melanie Perkins, Patrick Collison, Ryan Breslow, Mo Ibrahim, Falguni Nayar, Bolaji Akinboro, Isabelle Kocher de Leyritz, Nicola Mendelsohn CBE, Brian Chesky, Dara Khosrowshahi, Hubert Joly, Hamza Mudassir, Liz Ryan, Raj Sisodia, Lynda Gratton, Reed Hastings, Demis Hassabis, Brian Armstrong, Fei-Fei Li, Henry Timms, Jeff Weiner, Aigerim Shorman Full directory at https://lnkd.in/g9WY7GFd If your leadership team needs more than a better reading list, I facilitate Working Genius workshops, executive team offsites, and keynotes for organisations globally. Email me at jonno@consultclarity.org.
posts.hook: Most LinkedIn CEO follow lists recycle the same 10 names.
posts.name: Liz Ryan

posts.text: Almost everyone is job searching the same way -- applying to posted jobs through recruiting websites that were never designed to help candidates, sending resumes written in language so identical and lifeless that hiring managers can barely tell one apart from another and waiting to be found instead of going out and finding the right people.

posts.text: Then the VP said, "You know, I was hired as HR Director here a year ago. I just got promoted to VP when our previous VP retired. To hit our goals for next year, our recruiting needs to be as strong as our product development or our sales organization -- which it has not been. We need the outlook you're talking about. Let me change my question: If I commit to do the things you outlined, will you take this job?"
posts.hook: Then the VP said, "You know, I was hired as HR Director here a year ago.
posts.name: Liz Ryan

posts.text: The traditional advice for getting a job doesn’t work, either – from old-school robotic branding like, “Motivated self-starter with a proven track record” to the misguided belief that a job search is just a numbers game - so that if you apply for 40 jobs and don’t hear anything back, the best thing to do is use the same resume to apply for another 100 jobs.

posts.text: I started writing and speaking about these ideas, and now there are three million of us in the Human Workplace movement to reinvent work for people. For about 25 years I've been teaching how to get a job that deserves your talents, how to brand yourself for the roles you actually want, and how to use Pain Letters™ and your Human-Voiced Resume™ full of Dragon-Slaying Stories™ to stand out — and to remind yourself of the triumphant journey you've already taken.
posts.hook: I started writing and speaking about these ideas, and now there are three million of us in the Human Workplace movement to reinvent work for…
posts.name: Liz Ryan

posts.text: 4. You can’t lead with what you’ve done. That’s table stakes. At this level, you’re hired for your point of view. You're hired into an exec role because your thinking and their thinking about the industry, function and landscape align.
posts.name: Liz Ryan

posts.text: That voice is a lie. But it’s a convincing one when you’re navigating a process that wasn’t built for someone at your level — that asks you to shrink yourself into an application, that offers nothing back but silence, that measures your 30 years of impact against a keyword list someone in HR built on a Tuesday afternoon.
posts.name: Liz Ryan
